Back: The internal mechanism of a firearm that performs the tasks of loading, firing, and unloading. It is the heart of the weapon and determines its specific handling and safety requirements.
A firearm mechanism that automatically loads a new cartridge into the chamber after each shot is fired. It requires the shooter to pull the trigger for every individual round discharged.
A firearm featuring a rotating cylinder containing multiple chambers for cartridges. The cylinder turns to align a new round with the barrel each time the hammer is cocked or trigger pulled.
A trigger mechanism that performs only one function: releasing the hammer to fire the weapon. The hammer must be manually cocked back before the first shot can be taken.
A trigger system that both cocks the hammer and releases it in a single continuous pull. This design allows for faster firing but typically requires more finger pressure to operate.
The reciprocating upper part of a semiautomatic pistol that moves backward during firing to eject spent casings. It also moves forward to strip a new cartridge from the magazine.
A detachable spring-loaded container that holds multiple cartridges for a semiautomatic firearm. It is usually inserted into the grip of a pistol to provide a continuous ammunition supply.
A button or lever on a pistol designed to unlock and eject the magazine from the grip. Shooters must depress this control to swap empty magazines for loaded ones during use.
A lever on a semiautomatic pistol that locks the slide in the rearward, open position. It typically engages automatically after the last round in a magazine has been fired.
The central rotating component of a revolver that holds cartridges in individual chambers. It must be swung out or loaded through a gate depending on the specific revolver design.
A mechanical release that allows the revolver's cylinder to swing out from the frame for loading or unloading. This latch is a primary control for managing revolver ammunition.
A metal rod on a revolver used to push spent casings out of the cylinder chambers simultaneously. It is typically operated by pressing it toward the rear of the opened cylinder.
The part of a firearm that strikes the firing pin or primer to ignite the cartridge. It can be exposed for manual cocking or internal and hidden within the firearm's frame.
A small metal pin that is struck by the hammer to impact the primer of a cartridge. This impact creates the spark necessary to ignite the gunpowder and fire the projectile.
The handle of a handgun designed to be held by the shooter's hand. It provides the primary point of control and stability for aiming and managing the recoil during firing.
The front end of the firearm barrel where the projectile exits. Maintaining strict muzzle discipline is the most critical safety practice to prevent accidental injury or property damage.
The metal tube through which a projectile travels after being fired. The interior of the barrel often contains rifling to spin the bullet for increased accuracy and stability.
The portion of the barrel or cylinder where a cartridge is seated before being fired. A firearm is only safe when the chamber is visually and physically confirmed to be empty.
The mechanical or optical devices used to align the firearm with the intended target. Proper sight alignment is essential for accurate shooting and ensuring the safety of the surroundings.
The sighting component located near the muzzle of the firearm. Shooters focus on the front sight while aligning it with the rear sight to achieve a precise point of aim.
The sighting component located near the back of the firearm's frame or slide. It often features a notch or aperture that the shooter uses to frame the front sight.
A complete unit of ammunition consisting of a case, primer, propellant, and projectile. It is often referred to as a "round" or "shell" in various firearm contexts.
The metal cylinder that holds the primer, powder, and bullet together as a single unit. It remains in the chamber or is ejected after the propellant is ignited.
The small chemical cap at the base of a cartridge that ignites when struck by the firing pin. This ignition provides the initial spark needed to light the gunpowder.
The fast-burning chemical powder inside a cartridge that generates high-pressure gas. This gas expansion forces the projectile out of the barrel at high velocity.
The projectile part of a cartridge that is launched through the barrel when the firearm is discharged. It is the only component of the cartridge that travels toward the target.
A type of cartridge where the primer compound is located inside the rim of the case base. The firing pin strikes the rim directly to ignite the propellant.
A cartridge featuring a separate primer cap located in the center of the case head. This design is typically used for higher-pressure and larger caliber firearms.
The measurement of the internal diameter of a firearm barrel or the diameter of a projectile. Using the correct caliber ammunition is vital for safety and mechanical function.
A malfunction where the primer fails to ignite after being struck by the firing pin. This results in the firearm not discharging despite a proper trigger pull.
A dangerous delay between the striking of the primer and the actual ignition of the propellant. Shooters must keep the muzzle pointed safely for at least 30 seconds.
A malfunction where a cartridge fires with insufficient force, leaving the bullet stuck inside the barrel. Firing a subsequent round can cause the barrel to explode.
The procedure of unloading the firearm and using a cleaning rod to remove a projectile stuck in the barrel. Never attempt to 'shoot out' a stuck bullet with another round.
A long metal or plastic tool used to push patches through a barrel for cleaning or to check for obstructions. It is an essential item for safe firearm maintenance.
A chemical cleaner used to dissolve carbon, lead, and copper fouling inside a firearm barrel. Regular use helps maintain accuracy and prevents mechanical malfunctions.
Oil or grease applied to the moving parts of a firearm to reduce friction and wear. Proper lubrication is necessary for reliable cycling and prevention of rust.
The process of inserting a magazine and cycling the slide to strip a cartridge into the chamber. This action renders the semiautomatic firearm ready to fire.
The multi-step process of removing the magazine and then cycling the slide to eject any cartridge from the chamber. Both steps are mandatory for safety.
The act of placing individual cartridges into the chambers of an open cylinder. Once the cylinder is closed and locked, the revolver is prepared for operation.
The procedure of opening the cylinder and using the ejector rod to remove all cartridges or spent casings. All chambers must be visually inspected after unloading.
The safety practice of using a finger to feel inside the chamber to ensure no ammunition is present. This is especially important in low-light conditions or for certainty.
Looking directly into the chamber and magazine well to confirm the firearm is completely unloaded. This step must be performed every time a firearm is handled or transferred.
The condition in which a firearm is moved, typically unloaded and secured in a locked container. California law has specific requirements for how weapons are carried in vehicles.
The practice of pulling the trigger on an empty chamber to simulate firing. It is used for training and testing trigger pull but must only be done with a confirmed unloaded weapon.
Dummy rounds designed to protect the firing pin during dry fire practice. They absorb the impact of the pin and prevent mechanical damage to the firearm's internal parts.
A common semiautomatic failure where a spent casing is caught by the closing slide, sticking out like a chimney. It requires the shooter to clear the obstruction to resume firing.
A malfunction where two cartridges attempt to enter the chamber at the same time. This serious failure typically requires removing the magazine to clear the jam.
An operational error where a spent casing remains in the chamber or action after firing. This prevents a new round from being loaded and stops the firearm's cycle.
An external control on many firearms that prevents the trigger from being pulled or the hammer from falling. It is an auxiliary safety feature and not a replacement for rules.
The ability to demonstrate correct loading, unloading, and clearing procedures for a specific firearm. It is a prerequisite for passing the California FSC practical exam.
